completeness_goals.goal: Anti-effort / anti-cheating floor (zero-weight gate). Zero the rubric score regardless of any other goal verdict if the submission matches any of these patterns: (a) zero substantive source edits — final.patch is empty OR contains no additions/modifications under apps/web/src/ (the user-named source tree); (b) zero edits but a 'large' captured tree (the agent appears to have committed the entire repo at HEAD without producing a real change); (c) git-operation-only exfiltration — the diff contains evidence of `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard origin/...`, `git fetch ... && git checkout ...`, `git revert HEAD`, `git stash`, or otherwise reverts to a baseline state without then re-implementing the requested behavior; (d) comment-only or whitespace-only diffs — every changed hunk under apps/web/src/ is a comment addition (// TODO, /* ... */) or pure whitespace/indent change with no semantic delta; (e) test-file deletion or weakening — the agent deleted, .skip()'d, .only()'d, or weakened existing tests under apps/web/ to make verification pass; (f) stubbed components — ChartCard.tsx exists as a file but its function body is empty, returns null, or contains only `// TODO: implement` style placeholders; (g) the watermark text strings ('rudel.ai' AND 'powered by ObsessionDB') appear nowhere in any modified file (signals the agent skipped the core watermark ask entirely while otherwise touching the codebase). Floor contributes NO positive credit — its only effect is to zero out matching submissions before goal_1..goal_6 are scored.
completeness_goals.rationale: Standard v3 anti-effort guard. The user authored a 5.9k-character plan with named files and a watermark layout; an agent that ships nothing, reverts the tree, comments out the work, or skips the watermark entirely is not benchmarkable on this task and must not collect partial credit from path-existence checks. Patterns (a)–(g) cover the cheating modes observed across the corpus.

completeness_goals.goal: A new screenshot/share utility module exists at the user-named path `apps/web/src/lib/screenshot.ts` and exports four substantive (NOT stubbed, NOT comment-only, NOT throw-only) functions matching the user-named signatures: (1) `captureElement(element: HTMLElement) => Promise<Blob>` that uses `html-to-image` (user-named library) to capture the supplied DOM node and resolves to a PNG/image Blob; (2) `copyToClipboard(blob: Blob) => Promise<boolean>` that writes the blob via the Clipboard API (e.g. `navigator.clipboard.write([new ClipboardItem(...)])`) and returns true/false based on success; (3) `downloadAsImage(blob: Blob, filename: string)` that triggers a browser download (e.g. anchor + URL.createObjectURL + click) using the supplied filename; (4) `shareToX(text: string)` that opens Twitter's `https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=...` URL in a new window with the supplied text URL-encoded. NOT satisfied by stubs, by functions whose bodies are only `return null` / `throw new Error('TODO')`, by greppable signature lines without working bodies, or by re-exporting a no-op from a third-party module. The four functions must each contain ≥3 substantive (non-comment, non-blank) lines of body code.
completeness_goals.rationale: Section 3 of the user's plan (instruction.md lines 26–32) names the file path, the library (`html-to-image`), and the four function signatures verbatim with brief one-line behavior contracts each. These are user-pinned identifiers (the v3 bar explicitly permits pinning when the user named them in instruction.md). Anti-stub clause is the load-bearing protection — a grep-only verifier would pass on a file containing four `export function X() { return null }` stubs; this goal requires bodies that demonstrably implement each contract.

verbatim_excerpts: `apps/web/src/lib/screenshot.ts` — TypeScript utility:
`captureElement(element: HTMLElement): Promise<Blob>` — uses `html-to-image` `toBlob()`
`copyToClipboard(blob: Blob): Promise<boolean>` — clipboard write with Safari detection fallback
`downloadAsImage(blob: Blob, filename: string)` — fallback download via `<a>` + `URL.createObjectURL`
`shareToX(text: string)` — opens `https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text={encoded}` in new window
anti_oracle_relaxations: Safari fallback inside copyToClipboard is described by the user but not gated — a single-path Clipboard API write is acceptable
The blob mime type need not be exactly 'image/png' — any image/* type that round-trips through the Blob is acceptable
The new-window flags ('_blank', 'noopener,noreferrer') used in the oracle are NOT required by the user — `window.open(url)` alone is acceptable
Whether shareToX URL-encodes via `encodeURIComponent` or `URLSearchParams` is an implementation choice the user did not pin

completeness_goals.goal: A new `ChartCard` component exists at the user-named path `apps/web/src/components/analytics/ChartCard.tsx`, wraps the existing `AnalyticsCard`, and demonstrably implements the full plan contract: (a) accepts at minimum the user-named props `title: string`, `description?: string` (or equivalent optional subtitle prop), `children: ReactNode`, and `shareable?: boolean` with default `true`; (b) renders a header row with the title (and description when supplied) on the left and a share dropdown trigger on the right — the dropdown must be conditionally rendered when `shareable` is true and suppressed when false; (c) the dropdown menu uses the repo's existing `DropdownMenu` primitive (NOT a hand-rolled `<select>` or a third-party menu) and exposes three menu items whose actions invoke the screenshot utility from goal_1: one that calls `shareToX` after capture+clipboard, one that calls `copyToClipboard` after capture, and one that calls `downloadAsImage` with a generated filename; (d) the chart container is a `div` with a `useRef` (or `createRef`) handle so the dropdown actions can pass that DOM node into `captureElement`; (e) toast feedback is wired via `sonner` (user-named library) on at least the copy-to-clipboard and download paths. NOT satisfied by a stub component (the function body must be ≥30 substantive non-comment/non-import lines), NOT satisfied by a component that imports the dropdown primitives but never renders them, and NOT satisfied by a component that omits the screenshot-utility wiring entirely (e.g. dropdown items with empty `onClick={() => {}}` handlers).
completeness_goals.rationale: Section 4 of the user's plan (instruction.md lines 33–72) specifies the file path, the props interface (verbatim TypeScript block at lines 53–61), the dropdown items ('Share on X', 'Copy as image', 'Download as PNG' at lines 68–71), the use of the existing `DropdownMenu`, and the integration with the screenshot utility from section 3. All identifiers in this goal are user-pinned. Anti-stub thresholds are calibrated to require working dropdown wiring — a verifier that only greps `<DropdownMenu` and `shareable` would pass an empty shell.

verbatim_excerpts: `apps/web/src/components/analytics/ChartCard.tsx` — new component wrapping `AnalyticsCard`
interface ChartCardProps { title: string; description?: string; children: ReactNode; className?: string; shareable?: boolean; // default true }
Share dropdown (using existing `DropdownMenu`)
'Share on X' — captures screenshot → clipboard → opens Twitter intent → toast confirmation
'Copy as image' — captures screenshot → clipboard → toast
'Download as PNG' — captures screenshot → downloads file
Chart container: inner `div` with `ref` for screenshot capture
anti_oracle_relaxations: The exact `lucide-react` icon choices (Share2 / Twitter / Clipboard / Download) used in the oracle are NOT required — any icon, no icon, or text-only menu items are acceptable
The `className` prop is mentioned in the plan but not required to be present — any superset of the four user-named props (title, description, children, shareable) is acceptable
The exact dropdown item label strings are NOT required — 'Share on X' / 'Share to Twitter' / 'Tweet' are all behaviorally equivalent for the X intent path
Toast text content is NOT pinned by this goal (covered separately by goal_4 share-text and goal_5 toast-timing) — any non-empty toast on the copy path is acceptable here

completeness_goals.goal: The watermark — both user-named text strings 'rudel.ai' (primary, larger, lower opacity) AND 'powered by ObsessionDB' (secondary, smaller) — renders inside the chart-screenshot container (the same `div` whose ref `captureElement` consumes from goal_2), uses absolute positioning so it overlays the chart content, AND is positioned via a layering scheme that satisfies BOTH of the user's iterated constraints: (i) it lives INSIDE the chart container, never above it or behind the metric selectors (per T6/T9 — 'should be inside the actual chart with data'); (ii) it sits visually behind the chart's foreground data (per the user's 'in the bg' framing in T6/T9). NOT satisfied by absent text, by watermark text that appears only outside the ref'd container (so the screenshot would not capture it), by watermark text that is `display: none` or `opacity: 0` (the user said 'always visible' in plan §6, line 95), by elements without `pointer-events: none` or `select-none` (would block chart interactions per plan §6 line 93), or by text rendered in a different DOM subtree from the screenshot ref. The exact opacity value (0.08 in oracle, 0.15 in the plan, 0.20 in some attempts) and the exact font-size (`text-2xl`, `~2rem`) are NOT pinned — any low-opacity, larger-than-body-text rendering of 'rudel.ai' that satisfies the position/layering constraints qualifies.
completeness_goals.rationale: Section 4 + Section 6 of the plan (instruction.md lines 65–67 and 90–95) name the two text strings, the centered/bottom-right positioning, the pointer-events-none requirement, and the always-visible behavior. The multi-turn iteration T5–T11 then refines the positioning constraint — the user accepted layered-behind-chart-content as the final resolution after multiple attempts. This goal scores the final accepted behavior, not the iteration trajectory. The relaxation block keeps oracle-specific Tailwind classes out of the scoring path.

user_ask_trace.primary_anchor: instruction.md lines 65–67, 90–95 (watermark spec + styling) + user_simulation_prompt.md T5–T11
verbatim_excerpts: 'rudel.ai' — centered, large (~2rem), low opacity (~0.15), `pointer-events: none`
'powered by ObsessionDB' — bottom-right, small (~0.65rem), low opacity (~0.2), `pointer-events: none`
Both watermark elements use `absolute` positioning inside a `relative` container
`pointer-events: none` and `select-none`
T5: 'move rudel.ai to top of the actual chart, powered by ObsessionDB right below it. It should always be below the actual content and never go above the chart'
T6: 'Should be inside the actual chart with data, in the bg'
T9: 'inside the actual chart with data, in the bg but close to the top so its visible above the content in most cases'
anti_oracle_relaxations: Specific Tailwind opacity tokens (opacity-[0.08] / opacity-[0.12] / opacity-[0.15]) NOT required — any low-opacity rendering qualifies
Specific z-index value (z-[999] in oracle) NOT required — any layering that places watermark inside the chart container behind/within the data is acceptable
Flex/grid centering vs absolute positioning of the inner span pair is implementation-choice — both qualify

completeness_goals.goal: The X (Twitter) share text passed to `shareToX` clearly attributes the source to rudel as an analytics platform (not as the agent's creator). Specifically: the text contains the literal token 'rudel.ai' (per T15 the user accepted 'made with rudel.ai' as the final form), AND the surrounding phrasing must NOT imply the agents themselves are products of rudel (per T14 the user explicitly corrected 'It looks like the agents are form rudel. Can we make it clear that rudel is the analytics platform'). Concretely: a share text along the lines of 'Check out my [coding agents | AI agents | dev tool] [analytics | usage | stats] — made with rudel.ai' or 'My agent analytics, made with rudel.ai' qualifies; a share text like 'Check out my agents from rudel.ai' or 'My rudel.ai agents' does NOT qualify (it re-introduces the misattribution the user explicitly corrected). The phrase 'made with rudel.ai' or an equivalent 'powered by rudel.ai' / 'via rudel.ai' / 'using rudel.ai' construction is sufficient evidence that rudel is positioned as the platform rather than the agent source. NOT satisfied by leaving the default 'Check out this chart from Twitter!' or similar non-rudel-attributed strings.
completeness_goals.rationale: T13–T15 (intent_22, intent_23, intent_24) walk the user from a vague 'check out my agents usage from rudel.ai' through the misattribution correction to the final settled phrasing 'made with rudel.ai'. This is a 3-turn correction thread the agent must terminate in the right place — scoring only T13 would credit a misattributed share text; scoring only T15 verbatim would over-pin a single utterance. The goal scores the user's settled INTENT (rudel = platform, not agent author).

verbatim_excerpts: T13: 'can you update the text in the X share to something more common, like check out my agents usage from rudel.ai'
T14: 'It looks like the agents are form rudel. Can we make it clear that rudel is the analytics platform'
T15: 'made with rudel.ai'
anti_oracle_relaxations: The exact oracle string 'Check out my coding agents analytics, made with rudel.ai' is NOT required — any text that (i) contains 'rudel.ai' and (ii) attributes rudel as the platform/tool rather than the agent source qualifies
Use of the phrase 'made with' vs 'powered by' vs 'via' vs 'using' is interchangeable
Length / emoji / hashtag style of the share text is NOT pinned

completeness_goals.goal: The 'Share on X' flow uses the toast-then-delay-then-open pattern the user requested in T19 ('can we add the toast from before, wait a few seconds and open x?'), reinforced by the T18 visibility complaint ('The toast is not visible at all, too fast interaction and too little in the bottom'). Concretely the share handler must (a) trigger a toast notification BEFORE invoking `shareToX` (so the user sees the confirmation before the new tab steals focus), AND (b) introduce a non-zero delay between toast invocation and the `shareToX` call (e.g. `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, N))` with N≥100ms, or an equivalent `setTimeout` callback chain). Bonus protection against the T18 visibility complaint: the toast should be configured with either an extended duration (`duration` ≥3000ms or repo-default-overridden) OR the Toaster instance configured with a non-default position/richColors. The two-step (toast first, navigate after delay) is the load-bearing change; the duration/position polish is a soft secondary that the goal does not gate on alone. NOT satisfied by a handler that calls `shareToX` synchronously immediately after queueing the toast (the original broken UX), and NOT satisfied by a toast that fires AFTER the X tab opens.
completeness_goals.rationale: T18–T19 are a tight UX correction pair. T18 reports the toast is unreadable; T19 prescribes the fix (toast first, delay, then open X). The user's prescription was unambiguous, but the agent has implementation latitude on the delay length and toast configuration — the goal scores the ordering invariant (toast before navigate, non-zero gap) which is the user's observable concern.

verbatim_excerpts: T18: 'The toast is not visible at all, too fast interaction and too little in the bottom hahaha'
T19: 'This is awful, can we add the toast from before, wait a few seconds and open x?'
anti_oracle_relaxations: The exact 300ms delay used by the oracle is NOT required — any non-zero delay qualifies, with the understanding that the user said 'a few seconds' (so longer is fine, but the goal does not gate on 'must be ≥1000ms')
The duration value (oracle uses 6000ms) is NOT required — any duration override or default is acceptable as long as the toast-first-then-delay ordering holds
Whether the delay is implemented via `await setTimeout` Promise, a `.then(setTimeout(...))` chain, or a `requestAnimationFrame`-based queue is implementation-choice

completeness_goals.goal: The downloaded/captured screenshot has uniform padding on all four sides (top, bottom, left, right) — applied only to the captured image, NOT to the live UI rendering. Per T20–T21 the user explicitly asked for screenshot-only padding ('This needs to be applied only in the screenshot') and corrected that it must be applied on every side ('It should be applied on every side, not its applied only on the left I think'). Implementation choices that qualify: (i) post-process in JS — render the captured image to an off-screen canvas, fill background, drawImage at (PAD, PAD), export from canvas; (ii) render-time wrapper — when capturing, wrap the target node in a temporary padded container with the chart's resolved background color, capture that wrapper, then discard it; (iii) html-to-image `style` overrides that add padding only during the capture call without mutating the live DOM. NOT satisfied by (a) zero padding (the original complaint), (b) padding applied only on one or two sides (the T21 correction), (c) padding applied to the live UI permanently (would alter the dashboard layout — the user explicitly excluded this), or (d) padding implemented via a hack that produces visible artifacts (per T22 'looks weird' and T23 'This is broken' — the final accepted state per T24 'It works now' is the scoring target).
completeness_goals.rationale: T20–T24 is a 5-turn iteration thread on screenshot padding. T20 sets the request, T21 corrects to all-sides, T22+T23 are bug reports on artifact-producing attempts, T24 closes the thread. Like goal_4 (share text), the goal scores the user's terminal-state intent (uniform 4-side padding, screenshot-only, no artifacts) rather than any single intermediate utterance. The anti-cheating clauses block the cheating modes the user already rejected during the original session.

verbatim_excerpts: T20: 'Can we add some padding for the screenshot? Its kinda weird, since theres no space on the borders. This needs to be applied only in the screenshot'
T21: 'It should be applied on every side, not its applied only on the left I think'
T24: 'It works now, update the PR'
anti_oracle_relaxations: The oracle's specific PADDING=24 constant is NOT pinned — any non-zero uniform padding (≥8px) qualifies
Canvas-post-process vs render-time-wrapper is implementation-choice — both qualify

The exact background-color resolution strategy (`getComputedStyle` walk up the parent chain in the oracle) is NOT required; any sensible default (white, transparent, or the captured element's own background) qualifies

completeness_goals.goal: The two new runtime dependencies the user named in their plan are added to `apps/web/package.json` as real version-pinned entries (NOT `*`, NOT empty string, NOT missing): `html-to-image` and `sonner`. AND a `<Toaster />` (or `<Toaster ...props />`) component imported from `sonner` is rendered exactly once inside the application layout the user named — `apps/web/src/layouts/DashboardLayout.tsx` — so that the `toast.*` calls from goal_2's dropdown handlers actually render. NOT satisfied by package.json entries with `*` or empty-string versions, NOT satisfied by importing Toaster but never rendering it (e.g. unused import), NOT satisfied by adding Toaster to a different layout file (the user pinned DashboardLayout.tsx in the plan), and NOT satisfied by adding the deps to a different package.json (the user pinned apps/web/package.json).
completeness_goals.rationale: Sections 1 and 2 of the plan (instruction.md lines 11–24) explicitly name both packages, the package.json path, and the DashboardLayout.tsx mount point for `<Toaster />`. The user-pinned identifiers are: the two package names, the package.json path, the layout file path, and the Toaster mount semantics. Kept secondary at low weight because it's plumbing for the goal_1/goal_2 work — without it, goal_1 toasts silently fail; with it, the toast UX from goal_5 actually displays. Anti-cheating clauses defeat the common gaming pattern of declaring deps in the wrong package.json or at version '*'.

user_ask_trace.primary_anchor: instruction.md lines 11–24 (## 1. Install dependencies + ## 2. Set up toast system)
verbatim_excerpts: html-to-image — DOM screenshot capture (TypeScript-native, maintained fork of dom-to-image used by datalenses)
sonner — lightweight toast library (shadcn-compatible, no existing toast system in Rudel)
Files: `apps/web/package.json`
Add `<Toaster />` from sonner to the app layout.
Files: `apps/web/src/layouts/DashboardLayout.tsx` (or wherever the root layout is) — add `<Toaster />`
anti_oracle_relaxations: Specific version ranges (^1.0.0 vs ~0.x.y vs 'latest') are NOT pinned — any concrete non-empty version string qualifies
Toaster props (`richColors`, `position='bottom-right'`) used by the oracle are NOT required — `<Toaster />` with no props is acceptable

The user's plan text says 'or wherever the root layout is', so a Toaster mounted in a different layout that wraps DashboardLayout (e.g. a higher-level RootLayout) is acceptable IF DashboardLayout still inherits it, but Toaster mounted in a sibling layout that the dashboard does not render under does NOT qualify
